title: Whether neighbourhoods should set their own pace of change
summary: Argue whether residents of a district should have the authority to slow, pause, or refuse city-led development on their streets.
topic: Whether neighbourhoods have a right to refuse the pace of change imposed by city planners
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether residents of a particular neighbourhood — in a city such as Mexico City, Lisbon, or Hanoi — should have meaningful authority to slow down, pause, or refuse development projects that city-wide planners propose for their streets. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Anchor the argument in at least one named city, district, or programme. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What city-wide planning quietly assumes about the people who actually live on a given street is something most plans never make…
Granted, neighbourhood vetoes can be captured by the loudest and most propertied voices, and any genuine right of refusal needs…
In the Roma district of Mexico City, residents who have spent thirty years resisting the steady upmarketing of their streets have…

hintVocab.meaningVi: quyền phủ quyết của khu phố
quy hoạch toàn thành phố
bị áp đặt từ trên xuống
áp lực buộc cư dân phải dời đi
quá trình đô thị hóa cao cấp / thay máu khu phố
quá trình tham vấn
chiếm giữ một quy trình (cho lợi ích riêng)
dân chủ địa phương
biến ý kiến đóng góp thành chỉ mang tính tượng trưng
cư dân lâu năm
sự ổn định về quyền cư trú
duy trì quyền lợi cũ cho người đã có
đề xuất phân lại khu vực
được phê duyệt hình thức / không thực chất
phát triển từng bước
thời gian biểu của nhà phát triển
bị đẩy ra khỏi do giá cả
nhịp điệu của một con phố
quy hoạch địa phương có hiệu lực ràng buộc
lệnh tạm hoãn phá dỡ
có tiếng nói lớn và có tài sản

sampleText:
In any large city, a street is two things at once. It is a row of properties on a map a planner can rezone, demolish, or merge. It is also a daily working environment for the people who live, sell, sleep, and raise children along it. When those two perspectives come into conflict, the question of who decides the pace at which the street can be changed is not a technical one. It is a political one, and it has a clear answer most planning processes prefer not to make explicit.

What city-wide planning quietly assumes about the people who actually live on a given street is something most plans never make audible. The assumption is that the street can be improved, optimised, densified, or upmarketed at the city's tempo rather than the residents'. Consultation, where it happens, tends to be timed for after the major decisions have already been taken. Comments are received. Plans proceed. The residents who raise objections are treated as a kind of friction to be managed, not as the people whose neighbourhood is actually at stake.

Granted, neighbourhood vetoes can be captured by the loudest and most propertied voices, and any genuine right of refusal needs to be designed carefully. A wealthy enclave that uses its political power to block social housing nearby is exercising neighbourhood control for the wrong purpose. The case for slower, resident-led change is not the case for letting any group of homeowners protect their property values forever. The Roma district of Mexico City — where long-time residents, low-income tenants, and shopkeepers have together resisted certain forms of redevelopment — is not the same as a gated suburb refusing a bus route.

Even so, the difficulty of designing a fair process is not an argument for designing none. What a working neighbourhood-pace policy looks like, in practice, is more procedural than prohibitive: longer comment periods, binding local plans drafted with residents rather than for them, moratoriums on demolition where displacement is documented, and mechanisms that give long-time tenants — not only owners — a vote. Several Latin American cities have experimented with versions of this, and the experiments are imperfect rather than failed.

Admittedly, slower change has real costs. Housing that the wider city needs may be delayed. Infrastructure projects may stall. A neighbourhood that refuses all change can become a museum of itself. These costs are real. They are also smaller than the costs of being repeatedly told that one's home is, in the end, a line item on someone else's spreadsheet.

What residents are asking for is not a veto on the future. It is a hand on the dial that controls how quickly that future arrives. A city that cannot offer them that has not, in any meaningful sense, decided that they belong to it.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: What city-wide planning quietly assumes about the people who actually live on a given street is something most plans never make audible

sampleAnnotations.phrase: neighbourhood vetoes can be captured by the loudest and most propertied voices

sampleAnnotations.phrase: A wealthy enclave that uses its political power to block social housing nearby is exercising neighbourhood control for the wrong purpose

sampleAnnotations.phrase: What residents are asking for is not a veto on the future. It is a hand on the dial that controls how quickly that future arrives

sampleAnnotations.phrase: A city that cannot offer them that has not, in any meaningful sense, decided that they belong to it
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Một thành phố không thể cung cấp cho họ điều đó chưa, theo bất kỳ nghĩa có ý nghĩa nào, quyết định rằng họ thuộc về nó

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to expose an unspoken planning assumption
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What city-wide planning quietly assumes about the people who actually live on a given street is something most plans never make audible' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để đưa một giả định ngầm ra ánh sáng. Trạng từ 'quietly' và 'never' tạo ra một sự đối lập sắc bén giữa điều được giả định và điều được nói ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What city-wide planning quietly assumes about the people who actually live on a given street is something most plans never make audible' uses a what-cleft to bring an unstated assumption into the open. The adverbs 'quietly' and 'never' set up a sharp contrast between what is assumed and what is said aloud.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Multiple passives to foreground process rather than agent
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Consultation, where it happens, tends to be timed for after the major decisions have already been taken' dùng hai cấu trúc bị động ('be timed', 'have been taken') liên tiếp để đẩy chú ý ra khỏi người ra quyết định (vốn không tên) và đặt lên chính quá trình bị thao túng. Cách này tăng cường cảm giác rằng cư dân không thấy ai chịu trách nhiệm.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Consultation, where it happens, tends to be timed for after the major decisions have already been taken' uses two passives in sequence ('be timed', 'have been taken') to pull focus away from the (unnamed) decision-makers and place it on the manipulated process itself. This sharpens the sense that residents cannot see who is responsible.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong bất kỳ thành phố lớn nào, một con phố là hai thứ cùng một lúc. Nó là một dãy tài sản trên bản đồ mà một nhà quy hoạch có thể phân lại khu vực, phá dỡ, hay sáp nhập. Nó cũng là một môi trường làm việc hàng ngày cho những người sống, bán hàng, ngủ, và nuôi dạy con cái dọc theo nó. Khi hai góc nhìn đó xung đột với nhau, câu hỏi về việc ai quyết định tốc độ mà con phố có thể được thay đổi không phải là một câu hỏi kỹ thuật. Đó là một câu hỏi chính trị, và nó có một câu trả lời rõ ràng mà hầu hết các quy trình quy hoạch thích không làm rõ.

Điều mà quy hoạch toàn thành phố lặng lẽ giả định về những người thực sự sống trên một con phố cụ thể là điều mà hầu hết các kế hoạch không bao giờ nói ra. Giả định là con phố có thể được cải thiện, tối ưu hóa, làm dày đặc hơn, hay nâng cấp theo nhịp độ của thành phố chứ không phải của cư dân. Tham vấn, khi nó diễn ra, có xu hướng được sắp xếp sau khi các quyết định lớn đã được đưa ra. Bình luận được nhận. Kế hoạch tiến hành. Những cư dân đưa ra phản đối được đối xử như một dạng ma sát cần phải quản lý, chứ không phải như những người mà khu phố thực sự đang đặt cược.

Thừa nhận rằng, quyền phủ quyết của khu phố có thể bị chiếm giữ bởi những tiếng nói lớn nhất và có nhiều tài sản nhất, và bất kỳ quyền từ chối thực sự nào cũng cần được thiết kế cẩn thận. Một khu vực giàu có sử dụng quyền lực chính trị của nó để chặn nhà ở xã hội gần đó đang sử dụng quyền kiểm soát khu phố cho mục đích sai. Lập luận về sự thay đổi chậm hơn, do cư dân dẫn dắt không phải là lập luận cho phép bất kỳ nhóm chủ sở hữu nào bảo vệ giá trị tài sản của họ mãi mãi. Khu Roma của Mexico City — nơi cư dân lâu năm, người thuê thu nhập thấp, và chủ cửa hàng đã cùng nhau kháng cự một số hình thức tái phát triển — không giống với một khu ngoại ô có cổng từ chối một tuyến xe buýt.

Dẫu vậy, khó khăn trong việc thiết kế một quy trình công bằng không phải là lý do để không thiết kế gì cả. Một chính sách tốc độ khu phố hoạt động trông như thế nào, trong thực tế, là mang tính thủ tục hơn là cấm đoán: thời gian bình luận dài hơn, các quy hoạch địa phương ràng buộc được soạn thảo cùng với cư dân chứ không phải cho họ, lệnh tạm hoãn phá dỡ nơi việc dời cư đã được ghi nhận, và các cơ chế trao cho người thuê lâu năm — không chỉ chủ sở hữu — một lá phiếu. Một số thành phố Mỹ Latinh đã thử nghiệm các phiên bản của điều này, và các thử nghiệm là không hoàn hảo chứ không phải thất bại.

Thừa nhận rằng, thay đổi chậm hơn có chi phí thực sự. Nhà ở mà toàn thành phố cần có thể bị trì hoãn. Các dự án cơ sở hạ tầng có thể bị đình trệ. Một khu phố từ chối mọi thay đổi có thể trở thành bảo tàng của chính nó. Những chi phí này là có thật. Chúng cũng nhỏ hơn cái giá của việc liên tục bị nói rằng ngôi nhà của mình, cuối cùng, là một dòng mục trên bảng tính của người khác.

Điều mà cư dân đang yêu cầu không phải là quyền phủ quyết tương lai. Đó là một bàn tay đặt trên nút điều khiển tốc độ mà tương lai đó đến. Một thành phố không thể cung cấp cho họ điều đó chưa, theo bất kỳ nghĩa có ý nghĩa nào, quyết định rằng họ thuộc về nó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to expose an unspoken planning assumption?
options: Most plans audible never make what city-wide planning quietly assumes given street.
What city-wide planning quietly assumes about the people who actually live on a given street is something most plans never make audible.
Quietly assumes city-wide planning what about residents most plans never audible make is something.
Audible never make most plans city-wide planning what quietly assumes given street residents.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' brings the hidden assumption into the writer's spotlight, with the evaluation ('something most plans never make audible') in the emphatic position.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the writer's critique placed after 'is'.

options: Geological pressure that causes soil to shift under buildings
Economic and social pressure that forces existing residents to leave their neighbourhood
The pressure of more cars on a city's roads
The pressure of urgent deadlines on city planners
mcQuestions.explanation: Displacement pressure is the combination of rising rents, redevelopment, and lost commercial space that forces existing residents — especially low-income ones — to leave a neighbourhood they have lived in for years.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'displacement' mean — and what kind of pressure would cause it in a city?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'granted' correctly as a concession?
options: Granted, neighbourhood vetoes can be captured by the loudest and most propertied voices.
Neighbourhood vetoes granted by loudest propertied voices captured can be most.
Captured by the loudest voices granted neighbourhood vetoes propertied can be most.
Most propertied granted the loudest voices neighbourhood vetoes captured can be.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause cleanly grants a real point to the opposing view before the essay continues.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full, grammatical sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses multiple passives to foreground the manipulated process?
options: Consultation, where it happens, tends to be timed for after the major decisions have already been taken.
City planners time consultation after they take the major decisions where it happens.
Take the decisions consultation tends to be after where it happens for major timed.
Major decisions where it happens consultation taken been already to be timed tends for.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'be timed for' and 'have already been taken' are two passives in sequence, both removing the agents (planners, decision-makers) from view and foregrounding the process itself.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for two passive verbs in the same sentence — 'be + past participle' and 'have been + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to capture a process' mean in the essay's political sense?
options: To record a process on video for documentation purposes
To take control of a public process so that it serves a narrow private interest
To photograph a planning meeting for the local press
To formally adopt a process as part of city policy
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Process capture' is a political term: a procedure designed for broad public benefit is taken over — captured — by a narrow group whose interests then dominate it.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'capture' as 'take over and use for one's own purpose'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'a line item on someone else's spreadsheet'?
options: A wealthy investment property bought by a foreign owner
A small piece of data on someone else's plan, reducing one's home to a number rather than a place
A formal accounting entry in a city's budget
A row in a real estate database accessible to the public
mcQuestions.explanation: The metaphor reduces a person's home to a single bookkeeping entry — a dehumanising shrinkage the essay treats as the real cost of being denied any control over neighbourhood change.
mcQuestions.hint: What is being compared to what, and what is lost in the comparison?

options: Development that happens all at once across an entire city
Development that proceeds in small, stepwise additions rather than large-scale demolition and rebuild
Development paid for in instalments by a private investor
Development that requires increment in property taxes annually
mcQuestions.explanation: Incremental development is small-scale, gradual change — fixing one corner, replacing one building, planting one street — rather than wholesale clearance and reconstruction.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'increment' as a small step — what kind of development matches that?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a participle clause to describe how plans should be made?
options: Binding local plans drafted with residents rather than for them.
Drafted with residents binding local plans rather for them than.
For them rather than with residents binding local plans drafted.
Plans local binding rather than for them drafted residents with.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'drafted with residents rather than for them' is a reduced past-participle clause modifying 'binding local plans' — compressing a whole procedural model into a single descriptive phrase.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a past-participle phrase ('drafted with…') that follows and describes the noun.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the difficulty of designing a fair process is not an argument for designing none.
On the contrary, no process can ever be fair.
Furthermore, all wealthy enclaves must be abolished.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges the previous concession (that vetoes can be captured) and pivots to the writer's main point — design difficulty is no excuse for designing nothing.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's argument?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: Neighbourhoods should have an absolute right to block any change.
City planners should always override neighbourhood objections.
Residents — including long-time tenants, not only owners — should have a meaningful, procedurally protected role in controlling the pace of change in their neighbourhoods.
Only wealthy homeowners should be allowed to participate in planning processes.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues for procedural rights (longer comment periods, binding local plans, moratoriums where displacement is documented, votes for long-time tenants) — meaningful but bounded resident authority over pace.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph four for the writer's specific procedural proposals.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly people who live somewhere should basically get to say how fast it changes.
City planners are kind of dictators and it's pretty annoying for everyone, full stop.
Granting residents procedurally meaningful authority over the pace of redevelopment shifts a city's planning relationship from imposition to collaboration.
Mexico City has like a really cool district where residents totally pushed back on development and it worked.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('procedurally meaningful authority', 'pace of redevelopment', 'imposition to collaboration') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal intensifiers.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'basically', 'kind of', 'pretty', 'like', and other colloquial markers.

options: The strength of locks on a residential building
The legal and practical assurance that a resident can stay in their home without being evicted or priced out
An academic appointment that lasts for life
An investment account dedicated to housing purchases
mcQuestions.explanation: Tenure security describes how confidently a resident can plan to remain in their home — protected from eviction, displacement, or sudden rent rises that would force them out.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'tenure' as 'right to stay' and 'security' as 'protection of that right'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: A wealthy enclave that uses its political power to block social housing nearby is exercising neighbourhood control for the wrong purpose.
That uses its political power a wealthy enclave to block social housing nearby is exercising for the wrong purpose neighbourhood control.
Exercising neighbourhood control for the wrong purpose a wealthy enclave that uses its political power is to block social housing nearby.
Social housing nearby a wealthy enclave that uses its political power to block is exercising neighbourhood control for the wrong purpose.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'that uses its political power to block social housing nearby' is a defining relative clause specifying which wealthy enclave the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'that', 'which', or 'who'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'a museum of itself' suggest about a frozen neighbourhood?
options: A neighbourhood that has built an excellent local history museum
A neighbourhood that has stopped functioning as a living district and merely preserves its own past
A neighbourhood that has been formally designated a heritage zone
A neighbourhood that charges admission to outsiders
mcQuestions.explanation: The metaphor concedes a real risk: a neighbourhood that refuses all change can become a static display of its former self rather than a place where new life happens.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the difference between a 'living' place and a 'museum'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay with a precise reframing of what residents are asking for?
options: In conclusion, neighbourhoods deserve more attention from planners.
Therefore, all city-led development must immediately stop.
What residents are asking for is not a veto on the future. It is a hand on the dial that controls how quickly that future arrives.
To sum up, residents and planners both have valid points of view.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C reframes the demand precisely — not stopping change, but controlling its pace — in a memorable two-sentence parallel.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option states exactly what is being demanded, and what is not?

mcQuestions.prompt: What specific mechanisms does the essay propose for a neighbourhood-pace policy?
options: Higher taxes on developers and lower taxes on residents
Longer comment periods, binding local plans drafted with residents, moratoriums on demolition where displacement is documented, and votes for long-time tenants
Public petitions, online surveys, and televised debates
Mandatory relocation of all residents before redevelopment begins
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay lists exactly these four procedural mechanisms in paragraph four as the shape of a working neighbourhood-pace policy.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph four for the writer's specific procedural list.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a neighbourhood near you that has changed significantly in the last ten years. What was the actual decision process — who made the decisions, who was consulted, and who was treated as friction? If long-time residents had been given a real hand on the dial, what would have changed?
